# Mobile air quality monitors for urban air

The on-board air quality monitoring solution, using a network of devices installed in fleets of municipal vehicles, makes it possible to use the existing infrastructure in a city to obtain an **accurate and representative measurement of urban air quality**.

In this way, authorities can understand the impact of vehicle traffic on air pollution and thus **design and validate road transport strategies to improve air quality in urban areas**.

## Benefits of on-board monitoring

**Service fleets**  
This solution facilitates the measurement of pollutants on the road from moving vehicles by using on-board instruments in municipal or private vehicle fleets such as buses, taxis, refuse collection vehicles...

**Pollution trends**  
By using a vehicle's usual route, it is possible to identify where in the city there are higher concentrations of pollutants at different times of the day, and to monitor the evolution of pollutants as action plans are implemented.

**Hyperlocal air quality monitoring**  
This compact and mobile system enables hyperlocal air quality monitoring in unexplored areas of the city where standard solutions cannot be deployed and where highly representative data can be obtained.

## Urban air quality monitoring platform

Hyperlocal air quality data

Nanoenvi UrVAMM devices can be installed on **municipal transport infrastructure** (buses, garbage trucks and other service vehicles) that provide a continuous service and cover homogeneous and extensive routes, thus increasing the coverage of the data collected.

This allows **hyper-local monitoring of air quality in real time**, enabling public administrations to define control strategies and policies, manage traffic on critical routes and implement air quality improvement plans.

# Indoor and Outdoor Air Quality Monitor Sensors for Smart Cities

## Introduction to The Internet of Things (IoT) and Its Significance in Urban Planning

The concept of gathering data from sensors networks is not a novel one, such networks have been utilized for decades. It used to be the case that they required a lot of planning, their integration needed to happen from the start, one would not be able to easily add them to a system post its deployment.

Nowadays sensors have become so cheat and so easy to deploy and maintain (wireless, battery powered) that it is very easy to add them to any scenario that can benefit from gathering metrics in real time. New use-cases are enabled especially in large scale environments that are otherwise difficult to observe.

In the Urban planning landscape engineers now have access to a wealth of data that can be utilized to optimize how a city is maintained to offer better comfort to people and also help out with planning its expansion in a cost-efficient and sustainable way.

This article will delve deeper into some applications, devices and scenarios that are applicable to the smart city field.

### IoT and Its Role in Smart Cities

The concept of the Internet of Things (IoT) isn’t new, but its widespread applicability and importance have surged in recent years. At its core, IoT refers to the network of physical devices, from household appliances to industrial machinery, embedded with sensors, software, and other technologies to connect and exchange data with other devices and systems over the internet. The result is a more integrated and autonomous digital ecosystem, where devices can gather, share, and act upon data without human intervention.

Modern cities, with their vast infrastructure and diverse populations, are fertile grounds for IoT implementation. Streetlights, traffic signals, waste management systems, and even public transportation can be embedded with sensors to gather valuable data. This concept of integrating digital intelligence into the urban environment is often referred to as the “Smart City” paradigm. Here, the city’s infrastructure and services communicate in real-time, optimizing resources, reducing costs, and improving the inhabitants’ overall quality of life.

### Air Quality Monitoring As An Enabler for Proactive Measures

Among the myriad applications of IoT in urban environments, air quality monitoring has emerged as a top priority. With urbanization comes increased vehicular traffic, industrial activities, and construction, all of which can significantly degrade air quality. Poor air quality not only affects the health of residents but also has broader implications for the environment and economy.

Utilizing IoT for air quality monitoring means cities can have real-time insights into pollutant levels. This immediacy in data acquisition is pivotal for quick decision-making, allowing authorities to issue timely advisories, regulate industrial activities, or reroute traffic.

But IoT’s potential in air quality management isn’t limited to mere monitoring. With the vast amount of data collected, urban planners can employ advanced analytics and machine learning algorithms (ML) to predict future pollution spikes based on historical data, weather forecasts, and anticipated urban activities. Such predictive insights allow cities to be proactive, taking measures to prevent or minimize anticipated air quality issues before they happen.

## The Mechanism of Air Quality Sensors in IoT

Air quality sensors have become pivotal instruments in assessing and monitoring atmospheric pollutants. With the capacity to detect various gases and particulates, these devices offer insights that can inform policies, public advisories, and preventive measures. When integrated into the IoT framework, these sensors enable real-time data acquisition and analysis, rendering them indispensable tools in the modern urban setting.

### Overview of Air Quality Monitoring Techniques

Several methodologies drive the operation of these sensors, each suited to detecting specific pollutants:

### Deep Dive: Infrared Gas Sensing in Air Quality Monitoring

Infrared (IR) gas sensing stands out for its specificity and sensitivity. Here’s a detailed exploration of how it works:

### IoT in Indoor Air Quality Commercial Buildings and Offices

Commercial buildings and offices stand to gain significantly from the implementation of flood detection technologies. They can benefit even more than residential building for the following reasons:

![IoT in Commercial Buildings and Offices](data:image/png;base64,iVBORw0KGgoAAAANSUhEUgAABAAAAADkAQAAAAAu/GupAAAAAnRSTlMAAHaTzTgAAAAySURBVHja7cEBAQAAAIIg/69uSEABAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA8G5y5AABMvUfswAAAABJRU5ErkJggg==)![IoT in Commercial Buildings and Offices](https://media.ncd.io/20231220010243/NDIR-1024x228.png) 
![IoT in Commercial Buildings and Offices](https://media.ncd.io/20231220010243/NDIR-1024x228.png)

#### Principle

Every gas has a unique molecular structure that absorbs infrared light at specific wavelengths. When an infrared beam passes through a gas sample, the gas molecules absorb specific wavelengths, leaving “gaps” or “absorption lines” in the transmitted light spectrum. By identifying these gaps and quantifying the absorption, the gas’s concentration can be determined.

#### Components

An infrared gas sensor typically consists of an **IR light source,** a *chamber or path* where the **gas sample flows**, a series of **optical filters** to select the *appropriate wavelength*, and a **photodetector** to measure the intensity of the transmitted light.

#### Measurement Process

The IR light source emits light that passes through the sample chamber. As the gas in the chamber absorbs specific IR wavelengths, the photodetector captures the transmitted light. The difference between the emitted and received light intensities correlates with the gas concentration.

#### Calibration and Considerations

Calibration is essential to ensure accurate readings. Periodic checks with known gas concentrations help in adjusting sensor readings. While IR sensors are robust, it’s crucial to ensure the optical components remain clean and free from obstructions or contaminants.

All of the aforementioned make for a much easier time when managing commercial property, which translates into cost and resource savings. A survey conducted by the Commercial Real Estate Development Association in 2023 revealed that properties utilizing IoT flood sensors saved an average of 15% in annual maintenance costs compared to those without. Furthermore, properties equipped with IoT systems saw an 85% decrease in water-related incidents. This more than makes up for the initial investment in provisioning the building with wireless smart sensors.

### Advantages of NDIR Air Quality Sensors

There is a good reason for NDIR sensors to be so popular, as they offer a very good balance between performance and costs. There following characteristics are what makes this type of sensor so wide-spread.

#### Specificity and Stability

NDIR sensors are very selective. They can specifically detect certain gases without being affected by other gases present in the atmosphere. This is because each gas has a unique absorption fingerprint in the infrared spectrum and the filters are able to distinguish with a high degree of certainty.

In addition, these types of sensors provide very stable reading, their accuracy does not degrade over time. Some chemical sensors get poisoned and/or saturated, NDIR sensors have no such issues.

#### Calibration, Temperature and Humidity Stability

Once calibrated, NDIR sensors maintain their accuracy for extended periods, reducing the frequency of recalibration.

Advanced NDIR sensors can be designed to be less affected by ambient temperature and humidity variations, ensuring consistent readings across varying environmental conditions.

Overall, they are very stable over time, very accurate once calibrated and are not affected much by changing operation conditions.

#### Long Lifespan (No Consumable Parts)

NDIR sensors typically have a longer operational life compared to other types of gas sensors. They can operate for years without significant degradation in performance. This longevity of the sensing element coupled with up to 10 years of battery life for modern IoT Wireless Sensors (as per NCD specifications for all their sensors) translates into zero maintenance operation over a period of multiple years. This can add up to a lot of costs saving, time optimization and decreased service unavailability time.

Last, but not least this is also the result of the fact that they do not have consumable parts, which further limits the need for maintenance.

#### Fast Response Broad Detection Range

NDIR sensors offer a quick response time, which is essential for real-time monitoring and rapid detection of concentration changes. They are also very sensitive, able to detect gas concentrations from very low (parts per million) to high levels, making them versatile for different monitoring needs.

#### Low Power Consumption

Many NDIR sensors are designed to consume minimal power, making them suitable for battery-operated or remote applications.

#### Safety

As NDIR sensors operate based on infrared light absorption, they do not involve any chemical reactions. This means they pose no risk of explosions or harmful byproduct formation, making them safe for various applications.

While NDIR sensors provide numerous advantages, it’s essential to note that they are not universally ideal for detecting all gases. **Their efficiency and accuracy depend on the specific gas of interest** and its absorption characteristics in the infrared spectrum. Nevertheless, for gases like CO, they remain a preferred choice in many air quality monitoring applications.

**Infrared gas sensing offers a robust and efficient method for detecting specific gases in the urban environment.** As cities grapple with challenges like vehicular emissions and industrial pollutants, technologies like IR sensors, especially when integrated into the broader IoT ecosystem, become essential tools for timely and accurate air quality monitoring. Their long lifespan, efficient operation and the decreasing cost allow for the creation of large-scale IoT Wireless Sensor Networks, which paves the way for even more refined and responsive urban air quality management in the future.

## Smart Traffic Emissions Monitoring and Management (example use-case)

Let us look into an example use-case scenario where a Wireless IoT Air Quality sensor can be most beneficial. We provide an overview of the complete end-to-end solution, from the sensor itself, through the network to the cloud back-end.

### Overview

As society continuously develops so does traffic, people need transportation and this is causing cities to become more crowded. This has the negative effect of increased gas emissions from vehicles that pollute the air and cause significant degradation of the quality of life. This is especially severe in certain areas like metropolitan city center areas, where traffic is heavy 24/7, which cause a constant C02 emission level hike. Measures need to be taken and traffic needs to be regulated, not only based on road congestion but also on emission levels.

### Network Deployment

A network of sensors is deployed in strategic places throughout the city. The initial deployments can start at the busiest traffic junctions and continue on with smaller ones, adjusting as the system gathers more data. Additional sensors should be installed in underground parking spaces as it is vital to keep track of the air quality in confided spaces like these.

These are outdoor devices, so weatherproofing is important. Sensors should be at the very least IP65 rated and have robust exposure and solid mounting options. The [NCD Industrial Air Quality Sensor](https://store.ncd.io/product/industrial-iot-wireless-co2-temperature-humidity-sensor/) is a good example of these qualities.

![](data:image/png;base64,iVBORw0KGgoAAAANSUhEUgAAAXwAAAD9AQMAAACFsl5AAAAAA1BMVEUAAACnej3aAAAAAXRSTlMAQObYZgAAAAFiS0dEAIgFHUgAAAAiSURBVBgZ7cExAQAAAMIg+6feawhgAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAALzBtAAFnZ1VVAAAAAElFTkSuQmCC)
![](https://media.ncd.io/sites/2/20241007131105/Air-Quality-CO2-Particulate-THPC_A-1-380x253.jpg)

[Industrial IoT Wireless Air Quality CO2 Temperature Humidity Particulate Matter Sensor](https://store.ncd.io/?p=432393)

[View Product](https://store.ncd.io/?p=432393)

Additionally, the IoT sensors should utilize a networking technology that promote large scale deployment via long range, cost-efficiency and long battery life. **DigiMesh** and **LoRaWAN** are a good example, where the former has the advantage in network stability, ease of deployment and is self-healing (no single point of failure) and the latter is more cost-efficient and has better per device range.

The data is relayed via Gateways to the cloud where it is processed and analyzed. It is important to select to select devices that are compatible with a wide-range of platforms like [**AWS IoT Core**](https://aws.amazon.com/iot-core/)**,**[**Azure IoT Hub**](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/products/iot-hub)**,** [**Temboo’s Kosmos**](https://ncd.io/Temboo%E2%80%99s%20Kosmos)**,**etc. and have interoperability between vendors. NCD has solved this by making their API open so you can own your data and send it to any end-point where you are free to combine and analyze it in any way you want with the rest of the data in your IoT ecosystem.

### Device Configuration

Readings are taken over consistent intervals of time. As these devices are battery operated the time between transmission should be optimized for the best balance between battery life and data granularity. Measuring once every couple of hours or at the time of the heaviest traffic is a good strategy and could extend battery life up to 10 years (for NCD DigiMesh Devices).

Periodic calibration of these might be required, however good quality sensors come calibrated out of the box. This being said, opt for devices that support Over the Air (OTA) configuration and update in order to minimize the need for hands on operation (supported by all NCD nodes).

### Visualizing the data (Cloud platform)

This is a must in any good IoT deployment. Ingesting the data and visualizing it in a way that is conducive to making informed decisions is crucial to how effective a management and monitoring systems is going to be. In this particular example the best strategy would be to forward the data to the Cloud where it will be analyzed and visualized in a platform. A city is a large ecosystem with a wide area and many data points, compared to a smaller network that could be limited to a building or an area (factory, school, hotel, etc.).

There is a plethora of of solutions to store and process the data, platforms as AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, etc. as we mentioned in the last paragraph. You could also choose to host your own solution and/or go open source. This has the advantage of more control and better customization (to a degree as it requires more skill), however you get the burden of managing additional hardware and making sure you have solid uptime.

A good example of the aforementioned deployment would be using an [IoT Edge Gateway](https://store.ncd.io/product/enterprise-iiot-gateway/) that comes pre-provisioned with [Node-RED](https://nodered.org/). This makes it easy to parse and reformat the data and forward it over the web to an endpoint for example using HTTP or MQTT.

An [influxDB](https://www.influxdata.com/) instance stores the parsed data which can be analyzed and visualized with an in-house solution such as [Grafana](https://grafana.com/).

Another possibility would be to use an all in one platform that provides both storage, processing and visualization of the data. Good examples are [TagoIO](https://tago.io/) and [DataCake](https://datacake.co/), two very popular platforms that support a wide range of protocols (including Webhooks and MQTT) and have rule engines and a large selection of visualization widgets with a deep level of customization. They come at a cost but reduce the deployment time a lot and also remove the infrastructure management overhead.

In the end, which topology you choose would depend on the particular application, its size and the budget (skill) one has. A good start would be the MING stack (MQTT + influxDB + Node-RED + Grafana). Take a look at the article from [Balena](https://blog.balena.io/ming-stack-mqtt-influxdb-nodered-grafana-balena/), it is a good start.

## Implications for Urban Planning

Speaking from a practical perspective pollution is an issue, especially in larger cities, that has a direct and visible impact on the quality of life in these population centers. Having insights into CO concentration levels (a heatmap is a good representation) can be quite empowering as decision on future improvements can be made with confidence as they are backed by data.

**Policy formation**

Knowing which areas are most in need of improvement, city councils can make informed decision and create policies that specifically target certain areas. For example they can limit the use of vehicles in certain areas, in certain times etc.

I**nfrastructure decisions**

Knowing where pollution is heavier can be used to plan future infrastructure expansion. For example, parks can be created, greener spaces can be preserved in order to balance CO levels and filter out pollutants to create an overall better environment. Levels can be constantly monitored and plans adjusted as need be.

**Public awareness**

People get wrapped up in their daily lives, becoming ignorant on issue sometimes. Having real time data publicly available would created awareness of the state of the environment and giving them the choice to improve things on a personal level. This could lead to less car usage, avoidance of more polluted areas and overall awareness of the state of the environment in order to make decision that would positively impact it (if they choose to).

In summary having real time and history data on CO level empowers both communities as a whole and individuals to take action and focus their efforts in areas that are in most need of improvements.

## Conclusion and the Future

As urban centers continue to grow and evolve, the integration of technology, especially IoT and air quality sensors, will be instrumental. It provides not only a snapshot of the current state of the environment but also offers insights to anticipate future challenges and address them proactively.

With advancements in sensor technology and a growing emphasis on sustainable living, urban centers worldwide will undoubtedly lean more on such data-driven strategies. The synergy of IoT and urban planning holds the promise of cleaner, healthier, and smarter cities for future generations.

Highlights
----------

*   •Novel IoT-based system for real-time air quality monitoring in chrome plating industry. 
*   •IoT sensors monitor pollutants like NH3, CO, NO2, CH4, CO2, SO2, O3, PM2.5, PM10. 
*   •AI models (LSTM, Random Forest, Linear Regression) predict pollution levels accurately. 
*   •System activates exhaust fans based on predicted pollution, preventing hazards. 
*   •LSTM model achieved 99 % R² and 0.33 MAE for temperature and humidity forecasting. 

Abstract
--------

Air pollution in industrial environments, particularly in the chrome plating process, poses significant health risks to workers due to high concentrations of hazardous pollutants. Exposure to substances like hexavalent chromium, volatile organic compounds (VOCs), and particulate matter can lead to severe health issues, including respiratory problems and lung cancer. Continuous monitoring and timely intervention are crucial to mitigate these risks. Traditional air quality monitoring methods often lack real-time data analysis and predictive capabilities, limiting their effectiveness in addressing pollution hazards proactively. This paper introduces a real-time air pollution monitoring and forecasting system specifically designed for the chrome plating industry. The system, supported by Internet of Things (IoT) sensors and AI approaches, detects a wide range of air pollutants, including NH 3, CO, NO 2, CH 4, CO 2, SO 2, O 3, PM2.5, and PM10, and provides real-time data on pollutant concentration levels. Data collected by the sensors are processed using LSTM, Random Forest, and Linear Regression models to predict pollution levels. The LSTM model achieved a coefficient of variation (R²) of 99 % and a mean absolute percentage error (MAE) of 0.33 for temperature and humidity forecasting. For PM2.5, the Random Forest model outperformed others, achieving an R² of 84 % and an MAE of 10.11. The system activates factory exhaust fans to circulate air when high pollution levels are predicted to occur in the next hours, allowing for proactive measures to improve air quality before issues arise. This innovative approach demonstrates significant advancements in industrial environmental monitoring, enabling dynamic responses to pollution and improving air quality in industrial settings.

1. Introduction
---------------

### 1.1. Background

Air pollution is a pressing environmental issue with profound implications for human health and ecosystems, exacerbated significantly by industrial activities. Industrial emissions release a variety of hazardous substances, including particulate matter (PM2.5 and PM10), sulfur oxides (SOx), nitrogen oxides (NOx), carbon monoxide (CO), VOCs, and heavy metals, which degrade air quality and pose severe health risks ([Kumar and Rajput, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib25)). These pollutants contribute to respiratory and cardiovascular diseases, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), and even cancer, particularly affecting workers in industrial environments who are exposed to higher concentrations of these toxic substances ([Singh and Singh, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib46), [Hormati et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib17)). For instance, in the Upper Silesian Region of Poland, exposure to PM2.5 and PM10 was found to be several times higher than the national average, correlating with increased premature deaths and years of life lost due to air pollution ([Połednik, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib39)). Similarly, in Romania, significant concentrations of arsenic and PM2.5 were recorded, posing major health risks, especially to vulnerable populations like children ([Tanasa et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib47)). The adverse effects of air pollution extend beyond human health, impacting plant productivity, ecosystem biodiversity, and contributing to global phenomena such as climate change, ozone depletion, and acid rain ([Agrawal and Agrawal, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib1)). In mining areas, emissions from heavy motor vehicles and mining activities further exacerbate air pollution, leading to severe health issues such as lung cancer and respiratory disorders among workers and nearby residents ([Kumar and Rajput, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib25)). Effective regulations, policies, and technological interventions are crucial to mitigate these risks and protect both human health and the environment from the detrimental effects of industrial air pollution ([Singh et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib45)). Therefore, addressing industrial air pollution is imperative for ensuring sustainable development and safeguarding public health and ecosystems.

Despite the known dangers of air pollution in industrial settings, traditional air quality monitoring methods are often inadequate. These methods typically lack real-time data analysis and predictive capabilities, making it difficult to implement timely interventions. Consequently, there is a pressing need for more advanced monitoring solutions that can provide immediate feedback and predict future pollution levels to prevent hazardous conditions. This study aims to develop a comprehensive real-time air pollution monitoring and forecasting system tailored for the chrome plating industry. The primary objective is to mitigate health risks associated with high pollutant concentrations by providing accurate, real-time data and predictive insights into air quality. To address the limitations of traditional monitoring methods, we propose a system that integrates IoT sensors with advanced AI models. This system is designed to detect and measure various air pollutants, including NH 3, CO, NO 2, CH 4, CO 2, SO 2, O 3, PM2.5, and PM10, and to provide real-time data on their concentration levels. Our approach stands out by combining IoT and AI technologies to offer both real-time monitoring and predictive analytics. The system utilizes LSTM networks, Random Forest algorithms, and Linear Regression models to forecast pollution levels accurately. This predictive capability allows for proactive measures, such as activating factory exhaust fans before pollution levels become hazardous, thus ensuring a safer working environment.

### 1.2. Literature review

Air quality monitoring has evolved significantly over the years, driven by the increasing awareness of the detrimental effects of air pollution on human health and the environment. Historically, the need for air quality monitoring became evident after severe contamination events, which spurred the development of various technologies and methodologies to measure atmospheric pollutants ([Vélez-Guerrero et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib50)). Initially, monitoring efforts were rudimentary, relying on basic observational techniques and simple instruments. However, as industrialization, urbanization, and motorized transport surged, particularly in developed countries like the USA, Japan, and Germany, the complexity and sophistication of monitoring systems increased. These countries adopted automatic continuous monitoring instruments based on specific physicochemical or electrochemical properties of pollutants, although these systems were often too costly and complex for widespread use in developing nations ([Gupta and Singh, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib14)). The advent of the IoT has revolutionized air quality monitoring, enabling real-time data collection and analysis through interconnected sensors and devices. Modern systems now utilize a variety of sensors, such as MQ135, MQ6, MQ7, and MQ2, to detect harmful gases like NO 2, CO, NH 3, and benzene, and relay this data to microcontrollers and online servers for continuous monitoring and alerting ([Alekhya et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib2)). Additionally, innovative methodologies, such as the geospatial data analysis used in Cluj-Napoca, have improved the spatial and temporal resolution of monitoring networks, making them more efficient and cost-effective ([Camarasan et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib6)). In developing countries, simpler and more affordable monitoring systems based on USEPA concepts have been adapted to local conditions, ensuring broader accessibility and usability ([Gupta and Singh, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib14)). The integration of historical data and advanced modeling techniques has further enhanced the ability to estimate long-term pollutant concentrations, crucial for epidemiological studies on chronic diseases ([Michalik et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib35)).

Traditional air quality monitoring methods typically involve the use of robust, stationary monitoring stations that measure various pollutants such as CO 2, NO 2, NH 3, and benzene. These stations are highly accurate and provide reliable data, but they come with significant limitations. One major drawback is the high installation and maintenance costs, which restrict their deployment to larger cities and well-funded regions, leaving smaller cities and underdeveloped areas less covered ([Martinez et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib32)). Additionally, these traditional systems often require laboratory analysis, which results in poor precision and sensitivity in real-time monitoring ([Ganesh et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib10)). This lack of real-time data is a critical limitation, as it prevents timely responses to sudden changes in air quality, which can be detrimental to public health ([Kalaivani et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib22)). Furthermore, traditional methods lack predictive capabilities, making it difficult to forecast air quality trends and take preemptive measures. For instance, while HVAC systems can control indoor air quality, they often rely on extensive historical data to function effectively, which can be costly and may not adapt well to changing conditions ([Rescio et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib41)).

In response to these limitations, the advent of IoT-based air quality monitoring systems has emerged as a promising solution by providing real-time data with higher spatio-temporal resolution, although they come with their own challenges, such as lower accuracy and data quality concerns ([Buelvas et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib5)). These IoT systems use low-cost sensors and cloud-based platforms like ThingSpeak for data storage and visualization, enabling more accessible and widespread monitoring ([Karnati, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib24)). Despite their advantages, IoT systems still face issues related to data accuracy and the need for calibration to ensure reliable measurements ([Veerani et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib49)). Overall, while traditional air quality monitoring methods are reliable, their limitations in cost, real-time data provision, and predictive capabilities highlight the need for more advanced, cost-effective, and adaptive monitoring solutions.

Recent advancements in IoT technologies have addressed some of these challenges, significantly enhancing the effectiveness of air quality monitoring systems, providing real-time data and comprehensive analysis of various pollutants. IoT-based air quality monitoring systems utilize a range of sensors to detect harmful gases and compounds, such as CO 2, volatile organic compounds (VOCs), particulate matter, and other pollutants. For instance, the system designed by Hasbullah et al. employs sensors to measure temperature, humidity, CO 2, VOCs, and particulate matter, transmitting data to a microcontroller and notifying users via the Telegram application ([Hasbullah et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib15)). Similarly, Rahul A's study highlights the use of the Blynk app to fetch real-time pollution data from multiple locations, making it accessible on smartphones ([Naik et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib36)). Ziyodullayevna's research introduces a portable air quality detection device using MQ135 and MQ3 sensors, with data stored and visualized on the ThinkSpeak cloud-based web app, incorporating machine learning for data analysis ([Karnati, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib24)). The study by Kalaivani et al. discusses the integration of MQ2 and DHT11 sensors with the ESP32 microcontroller, utilizing the ThingSpeak platform for data logging and real-time alerts ([Kalaivani et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib22)). Ganesh et al. propose a three-phase IoT-based pollution monitoring system that tracks pollution levels using an internet server, providing real-time monitoring and long-term analysis ([Ganesh et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib10)). Veerani et al. present an advanced real-time air quality reporting system using Arduino IDE, gas sensors (MQ135), and GPS for live location tracking, with data integrated into a mobile app via IoT ([Alekhya et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib2)). These studies collectively demonstrate the effectiveness of IoT sensors in monitoring air quality, offering real-time data, remote accessibility, and comprehensive pollutant analysis, thereby contributing to public health and environmental protection.

AI and ML have become pivotal in air quality monitoring and prediction, leveraging various models to enhance accuracy and efficiency. LSTM networks and Gated Recurrent Units (GRU) are recurrent neural networks (RNNs) that have shown significant promise in predicting nitrogen dioxide (NO 2) concentrations, outperforming traditional methods despite challenges with data quality and generalization capabilities ([Cican et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib7)).

The integration of IoT devices with cloud-based AI models, such as the Bayesian-optimized hybrid time-series (BO-HyTS) model combining Seasonal Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (SARIMA) and LSTM, has further improved the accuracy of Air Quality Index (AQI) forecasts by capturing both linear and nonlinear data characteristics ([Ansari and Alam, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib3)). Model Output Statistics (MOS) approaches using machine learning algorithms like Random Forest (RF) and Gradient Boosting have been applied to downscale regional air quality forecasts, with the global approach often outperforming local models in predicting pollutants like particulate matter (PM10 and PM2.5), ozone (O 3), and NO 2 ([Bertrand et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib4)).

The deployment of low-cost sensors in hybrid networks, calibrated through data-driven ML methods, has also enhanced spatial resolution and accuracy in air quality monitoring, addressing the limitations of traditional, expensive monitoring stations ([Vajs et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib48)). AI models such as RF, Backpropagation Neural Network (BPNN), and Genetic Algorithm (GA) have been used to explore the correlation between air quality and meteorological factors, with fusion models like RF + BP + GA providing highly accurate AQI predictions ([Liu et al., 2023a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib28)).

In the context of health impacts, ML models including linear regression, RF, AdaBoost, and neural networks have been employed to study the relationship between air pollutant concentrations and pediatric respiratory diseases, with RF emerging as the best-performing model ([Ji et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib20)). A comprehensive review of ML models for air quality forecasting highlights the widespread use of deep learning models over the past decade, emphasizing their geographical distribution, predicted values, and evaluation metrics ([Méndez et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib33)).

Encoder-decoder architectures with attention mechanisms have been proposed for forecasting PM levels, demonstrating superior performance by focusing on the most relevant data elements and addressing challenges in missing data through adversarial networks for data augmentation ([Kalajdjieski et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib23)). The AIrSense framework exemplifies the use of AI for anomaly detection and data cleaning in low-cost sensor networks, ensuring reliable pollutant concentration measurements ([Rollo et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib42)).

Finally, the Genetic Algorithm-based Kernel Extreme Learning Machine (GA-KELM) has been enhanced to predict various pollutants and AQI, outperforming conventional models like convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and recurrent neural networks (RNNs) in terms of training speed and prediction accuracy ([Liu et al., 2023a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib29)). These diverse applications of AI and ML models underscore their critical role in advancing air quality monitoring and prediction, providing valuable insights for environmental management and public health.

IoT and AI-based air quality monitoring systems have seen various real-world implementations, significantly impacting pollution management and worker health. For instance, a distributed sensing system using commercial sensors was deployed to monitor toxic compounds in a melting furnace environment, effectively detecting dangerous conditions for workers and enhancing safety measures ([Parri et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib37)). Another notable implementation is the hybrid sensor network combining public monitoring stations with low-cost devices for air quality monitoring, which demonstrated improved calibration accuracy and cost-effectiveness, making it feasible for large-scale deployments ([Vajs et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib48)).

Additionally, an IoT-Cloud-based model using the BO-HyTS approach was proposed to forecast the Air Quality Index (AQI) in different meteorological conditions, providing accurate predictions that can inform policy decisions and environmental management strategies ([Ansari and Alam, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib3)). In industrial settings, a complete IoT-based air quality monitoring infrastructure was developed, integrating real-time data collection and machine learning techniques to predict safety level breaches, thus protecting both workers and the environment ([García et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib11)).

Despite these successes, several challenges and limitations persist. Sensor accuracy remains a significant technical challenge, particularly for low-cost sensors that are prone to errors and degradation over time. Intelligent calibration methods, such as those using Bayesian frameworks, have been proposed to address these issues, but they still face difficulties in maintaining accuracy during extreme events and detecting calibrator drift ([Zaidan et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib52)).

Data integration is another technical hurdle, as the high-dimensional, spatio-temporal data from numerous IoT devices require sophisticated models to analyze and forecast pollution levels accurately. Deep learning models with attention mechanisms have shown promise in this area, but they necessitate extensive computational resources and robust data augmentation techniques to handle missing data ([Kalajdjieski et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib23)).

Practical challenges include the cost and scalability of deploying these systems. While low-cost sensors offer a more affordable solution, their maintenance and calibration can be logistically complex, especially when deployed in large numbers ([Vajs et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib48)). The need for a flexible and modular calibration scheme, as proposed in the MAQ-CaF methodology, is crucial to ensure reliable and scalable air quality monitoring ([Hashmy et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib16)). Additionally, the rapid development of AIoT/IIoT/IoT technologies brings about challenges related to safety, security, latency, and interoperability, which are essential for the effective implementation of these systems in various applications, including Industry 4.0 and autonomous vehicles ([Hou et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib18)). Ethical considerations, such as potential biases in AI algorithms and the safeguarding of patient privacy in health-related applications, also pose significant challenges that need to be addressed to ensure the responsible use of AI in air quality monitoring ([Satapathy et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib44)). Overall, while IoT and AI-based air quality monitoring systems have demonstrated substantial benefits in pollution management and worker health, overcoming the technical and practical challenges is essential for their broader adoption and effectiveness. enriched_points.expanded_content: IoT devices enable continuous, real-time monitoring of air quality through sensors that detect various pollutants, allowing for immediate data collection and analysis.
Crowdsourced data harnesses community contributions to enhance air quality monitoring, providing diverse insights and facilitating localized pollution management strategies.
Remote sensing technologies, such as satellite imagery, provide large-scale air quality assessments, enabling the tracking of pollution trends over time and across regions.
Integrating big data analytics allows for the processing of vast amounts of air quality data, uncovering patterns and trends essential for effective urban planning and policy formulation.
